speaker: interviewee
text: Just right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Helen Granek. Tape number 4. Helen, we were talking about when you arrived in Sydney. Would you like to continue with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Sydney, and the next day we went to Melbourne. And I had one uncle living in Carlton and another one living in Elwood. And they had prepared a flat for us in Alma Road in St. Kilda near the corner of Hotham Street. And we lived there for a few months, and then we moved to a bigger flat in Ellesmere Road, Windsor off Williams Road. And I lived there till I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. I went to Windsor State School for a term. That was quite an experience. It was a very rough school, and I really wasn't in the right-- it wasn't the right spot for me. So I was then sent to Korowa Anglican School in Glen Iris, and I spent six years there till my-- till my VCE. And it was a-- a nice school. It was a good school. I got very good education there. But I was the outsider. It was a very WASPish school population. And in hindsight, I don't blame them for feeling reserve towards me. It really wasn't anti-Semitism. It was just they had never seen a different person and a foreign person. Migration had only just started. It was only about a year or 18 months after the war, and we were just on different levels. We were just on different planes. It would have been nice if I would have had a bit more friendship at school, but I didn't. But I made up for it straightaway. I made a lot-- quite a few friends-- Jewish friends-- who were neighbors, who were part of a crowd of friends that I was able to get into. And I was just so, so happy to be part of a Jewish crowd. The only other time I had had that feeling was after the war in Tschenstochau. I used to go for Hebrew lessons and religious instruction lesson to a synagogue in Tschenstochau, which was sort of working. And there was a group of Jewish girls who had no families who lived together. And they were quite observant, which I was very interested to find out all about that. And they were Zionists, and I believed that they were getting ready. It was like-- like a little mini-kibbutz, and they were hoping to go to Palestine, eventually to Israel. What happened to them, I don't know, because once we left, I lost contact. But I had this feeling, this wonderful feeling about being with Jewish people. And I had that again when I made all my friends in Melbourne. And then when I finished school, I went to university. And I straightaway joined the Jewish Student Society, and there I was really in my element. It was just wonderful to be with Jewish people. And as I grow older, I enjoy more and more being with Jewish people. And when I was about 18, when I was just-- I had just finished school, I met a young man. And there was straightaway something, some affinity, which I put down because we were both Holocaust survivors. We really didn't have to tell each other anything. We knew things about each other. We had this closeness straightaway. And we went out together for about three years. When I was 21, we got engaged on my 21st birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: John Granek. And we've just this year celebrated our 40th wedding anniversary. And we've got two wonderful sons, Michael, who's 36, who's an accountant, who married 10 years ago, but very tragically, he lost his wife. He's bringing up his eight-year-old son on his own. We're helping him. My grandson's name is Mark, and he's a beautiful child. And we've got another son who's 33, Ronnie, who lives in London, who's a beautiful human being, warm. Both my sons are warm, and both are very, very proud of their Jewishness. We're a very close family. And we just hope that we've brought our children and our grandson into a better world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, why have you decided to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just feel that it has to be told. Even if it'll stop a little bit of hatred or a little bit of intolerance or a little bit of bigotry, even on a very, very small degree, it'll mean that I've been part of something good, something positive. There are so many disbelievers and disclaimers in this world. And the far right are growing, that we have to do something to counteract that so that in future generations, carnage and intolerance and bigotry will go away, maybe. Maybe it won't continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has your experiences impacted on the way you've raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were probably a little bit over-protective. We were probably very ambitious, which I think Jewish parents are in general, but Holocaust survivors in particular. I hope it hasn't done them much harm. Over-protective, a bit nervous, concerned, maybe not letting go as much as other parents do. But in general, I don't think it's done our children any great harm, because there are so many positive sides to our upbringing, I think. Because they give us so much love and so much respect. And you'll hear my children speak later. I hope they will say what-- how they feel about it. Just going back, backtracking a bit, as I said, my cousin-- my 15-year-old cousin-- didn't survive the war. She was shot. And looking back now, I can imagine-- or I can't even imagine, thank God-- what it must have meant to her parents to have lost their only child. It's something they probably never recovered from. They probably blame themselves for letting her go with that young man, although at the time, it seemed the right thing to do. It must have been very, very hard for them to live with that. But they cared for me. My welfare was their prime concern as they settled into a new country. It was much easier for me to integrate. They had lived more than half a lifetime on the other side of the world. It wasn't easy for them to learn a new language, to fit  in to a new society. They didn't integrate as much. They kept with their Polish Jewish friends that they felt safe with. But that was all right. They made sure that I got the best education. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I gave them a lot of respect. I gave them love. And they eventually had two beautiful grandchildren that they adored. And they lived till their 70s and 80s, and they had a fairly comfortable life, and they still did things which they enjoyed doing. They traveled a bit. And now that whole old generation, bar one surviving aunt who is now 92, have gone, and we are the older generation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we've had 50 beautiful years in Australia, in this beautiful country. I haven't felt any anti-Semitism here. Maybe I've been lucky. Maybe it is there. I hope it's not. I hope it won't be. But I have found it to be a tolerant country, a country where if you want to do something, if you want to put in an effort, you can-- you can succeed. And it's-- we have a beautiful home. We've traveled. We've made wonderful friends, many of them Holocaust survivors. We've had a wonderful marriage. We're a lovely family unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other messages you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I'd just like to dedicate this whole video to my parents, and my grandmother, and my cousin, and all the other people who-- who didn't survive the war. I'm just lucky I'm a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for telling me  your story, Helen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, would you like to introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My husband, Johnny. JOHNNY (HUSBAND): Hi. As I said, we've been married for 40 years. My oldest son Michael. Our younger son Ronnie lives in London. And this is Mark, our beautiful grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something else you'd like to say, Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. What I'd left out before, a lot of people are frightened of getting old. To us, every birthday has not meant that we're getting old. It's been a celebration of life. We're just so thankful to be alive, to have lived over 50 years, raised the family, met-- did interesting things, and just loved life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. First of all, I have a message from my brother Ronnie, who, unfortunately, being in London, can't be here today. He says that he's very sorry for not being here today. He knows how important it is for both Mum and Dad, this whole filming. He wishes he would be there. He's put down a few thoughts to help with this project, and I'd like to read them on behalf of Ronnie and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'There is no one feeling or thought that one can have for a Holocaust survivor, whether it be one's parents or not. If a child of a Holocaust survivor--' sorry-- 'If, as a child of a Holocaust survivor, we are supposed to understand what our parents went through any more because it is-- it was a parent, this is also not necessarily true. As a child survivor, I am sure-- what I am sure--' can we start this again? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. 'There no one feeling or thought that one could have for a Holocaust survivor, whether it be one's parent or not. If, as a child of a Holocaust survivor we are supposed to understand what our parents went through any more because it was a parent, this is also not necessarily true. As a child of a Holocaust survivor, what I'm sure is that we were taught about life differently. We were taught to appreciate every second of it. We were taught to do something with it. Parents of Holocaust children keep most of the truth away from their children as a safety net to protect them from the nightmares, the pain, and the truth. No parent would ever want their children to go through what they went through. If we have a few of the stories about the blackness of those six years-- I know of a few of the stories about the blackness of those six years, all of them now cherish and pasted onto those that were fortunate enough not to have been related to this episode in man's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: The expression on these people's faces is more painful than one could ever remember. Such is the impact of what went on, so terrifying for those who do not know. So what are the impact on those that you know? What are the effect on these people? It is truly horrifying to really think what Mum and Dad went through and survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mum and Dad, you are both amazing people. You were not beaten by these evils. You stood up and won, maybe by luck, but you won. This is what you and the rest of us must always remember. Whatever you went through, you survived and won. This has made you the driving force behind the rest of us, because no matter how bad it was for you, you went on with your lives and made something of it. You also made sure our lives as children were protected from any possibility of evil, that we will always be-- that we will always be taken care of better than ever, though possible, and that we always will be strong and safe, no matter where-- no matter where we were in the world. I know this every time I walk out into the world. This is why we can survive and fight for everything we have. The pain and suffering you have endured has given us strength and protection to continue and succeed. Out of the immense bad that you have suffered comes immense good. Please do not forget this, because as parents, this is all I can think-- this is all I think you ever wanted for your children. Please know it worked. You did and do protect us from evil, and you did teach us how to survive. Thank you.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I like it when [INAUDIBLE] to kick it with me. It's always fun. And I always take marks on everything. And nana, both of you, I like-- I like how you spoil me and all that. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And they love me all the time, and I love them. And that's really all I want to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe this photo for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my mother and my father and myself. I was-- I look like I was probably about three years old. As I said, we always used to go out on outings on Sundays with my parents or just my father and I. And as far as I remember, this was my first introduction to theater, to art, to music, which has been with me for all my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Solomon, known as Monia, and my mother's name was Rivkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how  did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my parents-- my father had a brother and a sister living in Australia. And he must have sent quite a few, because there are quite a few photographs of-- sort of similar photographs to this one, which I got from my uncles when I arrived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who is in  this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother, my father's mother. Her name was Faiga Zylberblat, nee Tyrocki. I remember her as a very gracious lady, very well-groomed. She loved to wear collars, lace collars, scarves, brooches. And although she was very shortsighted, she always looked immaculate, and very-- even to the last day that I remember her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately when this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There may be one-- there may be something on the other side of it. But I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: Would have been in the 1930s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get  this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as-- as with the other photo, all these photos were sent-- probably my grandmother sent it to her son or her daughter in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, could you describe this photo for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's a photo of my father and my mother walking, probably in one of the streets of Warsaw. As you see, my father is wearing the latest gear that was fashionable in the 1930s. And I think you can see that my parents were reasonably well-off, because they're well dressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my aunt, who became my adopted mother. Her name was Luba Treyvish. As far as I remember, this photo was taken not very long after the war. And as you see, she's very well preserved. The scars that she carried were mostly on the inside. Even in-- even in the camps, she managed to look tidy and clean and reasonably well-dressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who's  in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my uncle, my adopted father, Dr. Eugene Treyvish. He was one of the most handsome men I have ever known. He's got this beautiful smile, very kind man. He loved me very much, and he was always very good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, could you describe this photo for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On the right-hand side is my late cousin, Hanecka Treyvish, with me. She-- I would have been about four or five, and she would have been about 12, 13 at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. I think it was sent-- well, actually, we would have been a bit younger, because it would have been sent before 1939. So she would have been about 11, 12, and  I would have been about three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who's this person in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my cousin Hanecka as a-- as a younger child. She was just very beautiful, very talented dancer and very, very artistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would this have been taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early 1930s, I would think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, could you describe this photo for  me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Johnny's and mine wedding photo. We were married on the 2nd of February, 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was  this photo taken place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which synagogue were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Temple in Alma Road. And then we had our reception at the Dorchester.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest son, Michael. He would have been about four, four and a half, or five in that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Ronnie, my younger son. I think he was about 18 months in this-- when this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my two sons taken two and a half years ago in London. Michael is in the navy T-shirt, and Ronnie's in the white shirt. Michael visited Ronnie at that time in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Michael, my son, holding his little boy Mark. Mark there was about 12 to 18 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In their home in McKinnon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson Mark. He's eight years old. This is a school photo taken earlier this year. He turned eight in August. He is the most beautiful, warm, fun-loving boy, very cheeky at times, but very kind-hearted and very bright. And very-- And he loves his daddy very much, and I hope he loves us too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want her to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, could you describe this for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my landing permit to Australia. I arrived in Sydney on the 25th of November, 1946, and then went on to Melbourne, where I've  lived since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, could you describe this document for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a Certificate of Naturalization, my certificate, which happened about five years after we arrived. It would have been about '51, '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, would you like to tell me about the dress that you're holding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the dress which I mentioned in-- in the interview. This was the one that my aunt wore from-- when she left the ghetto, and she wore it right through in the camps. And it's actually quite well-preserved, seeing it's nearly 60 years old. And I mentioned that she was able to sew a diamond into, and this is the bow on this right-hand pocket where she actually sewed in the diamond. And that way, she-- she kept it, and the Germans didn't find it. So it's quite significant. This dress has been shown at a Holocaust exhibition years ago, and I feel it's quite valuable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you able to get this dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my aunt, when she passed away-- or before she passed away, she said to me, one day you will have it, and please keep it in the family, which I have. And although I've been asked to give it to the Holocaust Museum, I feel it-- it should be left in the family, along with a couple of other things, which I'm going to show now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, could you tell me about the bag that you're holding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This-- this is also miraculously preserved. My aunt wore this bag when she left the ghetto. And it was filled with, oh, comb, mirror, all her toiletries. But in between the rubbish, she was able to put some of her jewelry. And she hoped that it wouldn't be found, but eventually the Germans took all her jewelry away. But they-- she kept the bag, and this is it. And it's lovely leather and suede.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, could you tell me about what you're holding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a doll with a black face dressed in yellow and black, one shoe missing, but otherwise well-preserved. My aunt was given this by some young girls in the camp in Blizyn where we were. And for the rest of the war, she considered it her lucky mascot. So she's kept it, and she's also asked me to keep it in the family, which is very memorable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, could you tell me about the scarf that you're holding?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is absolutely amazing. This scarf belonged to my late grandmother, who in turn gave it to my aunt, because she liked it. It's-- it's about 60 years old by my calculation. It must have had a couple of tears at some stage, and the darning is just so delicate that you can hardly see it. It's beautiful. It's very, very fine, probably wool, and the colors are preserved. And it's just absolutely gorgeous. And I think I'm going to start wearing it from now on.
